name: Spriggan
description: Spriggans are mischievous and malevolent creatures from Cornish folklore, often described as small, wizened beings but capable of inflating themselves to gigantic proportions. They are associated with old ruins, barrows, cairns, and other ancient sites, acting as guardians or protectors of these places and the treasures they may contain. Spriggans are known for their spiteful nature and their tendency to cause trouble, including leading travelers astray, stealing from homes, and inflicting illness on livestock. They are also believed to be capable of controlling the weather, summoning storms and causing crops to fail. Despite their small size, they possess incredible strength and are often depicted as being surprisingly powerful. Some tales suggest they are remnants of an older, more powerful race, diminished over time. They are deeply connected to the land and resent any disturbance to it, making them formidable opponents to those who disrespect the ancient sites they guard. Spriggans are a constant reminder of the wild, untamed spirit of Cornwall and the dangers that lurk in the shadows of its ancient past.
subtitle: Guardians of the barrows and ruins
appearance: Spriggans are typically described as being small and wizened, with wrinkled faces and gnarled limbs. However, they possess the ability to inflate themselves to enormous sizes, becoming grotesque giants. Their appearance is often unsettling and reflects their malevolent nature. They are sometimes depicted wearing tattered clothing or adorned with natural materials like leaves and stones.
habitat: Spriggans are found in and around ancient sites in Cornwall, including barrows, cairns, standing stones, and abandoned ruins. They are particularly associated with areas that are considered to be liminal spaces, where the veil between worlds is thin.
behavior: Spriggans are generally mischievous and malevolent. They enjoy playing tricks on travelers, stealing from homes, and causing general mayhem. They are fiercely protective of their territory and will retaliate against anyone who disturbs the ancient sites they guard. They are also known to be vengeful and unforgiving.
weaknesses: Respect for ancient sites (if shown by others)

keyMyths.title: The Spriggan's Treasure
keyMyths.description: A farmer attempts to steal treasure from a spriggan's barrow and is met with terrible misfortune, losing his livestock and crops to a sudden storm summoned by the enraged creature. This reinforces the idea that spriggans protect the treasures within their barrows.

sources.author: Bottrell, William
sources.work: Traditions and Hearthside Stories of West Cornwall
sources.description: A collection of Cornish folklore and legends, including tales of spriggans.
sources.text: A collection of Cornish folklore and legends, including tales of spriggans.

sources.author: Hunt, Robert
sources.work: Popular Romances of the West of England
sources.description: Another compilation of Cornish folklore with references to spriggans and their mischievous nature.
